content:
14 min read
Just now

--

Bad names do not just make code harder to read. They hide weak ownership, wrong assumptions, and decisions that slow every future change.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Bad JavaScript names rarely break production directly.
They do something quieter.
They make developers misunderstand what the code owns, what it changes, what it returns, and what it is safe to touch.
That cost does not show up in a metric. It does not fail like a test. It does not appear as a red build, a crashed server, or a clear performance regression. It shows up in slower reviews, repeated questions, defensive debugging, duplicated fixes, and the same bug coming back under a different name.
That is why teams ignore it for so long.
A bad name feels cosmetic until the codebase grows around it. Then the name becomes part of the system’s language. Developers repeat it. Reviewers accept it. New features depend on it. Tests describe it. Bugs are discussed through it. Eventually, the wrong name becomes more than a small readability issue.
It becomes a wrong mental model.
This is the naming problem nobody measures in JavaScript. Not whether a variable should be camelCase. Not whether a function name should be shorter. Not whether a boolean should start with is, has, or can.
Those rules help, but they are not the real problem.
The real cost of poor naming is broken understanding. When names are vague, developers guess. When developers guess, they make unsafe changes. When unsafe changes spread, the codebase becomes harder to trust.
Naming is not decoration.
Naming is where engineering judgment becomes visible.

A Vague Name Usually Means the Responsibility Is Vague
The most expensive names are not always ugly.
They are often ordinary.
handleData. processUser. formatResponse. updateState. preparePayload. manageSession. checkAccess.
These names do not look broken at first. They are familiar enough to pass through review. They describe something real, but they describe it so broadly that the next developer has to open the function to understand what it actually owns.
That is the first hidden cost.
A vague name forces the reader to inspect implementation before they can trust intent.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In a small file, this feels harmless. In a real codebase, it becomes expensive because the same vague name can hide several responsibilities. A function called prepareUser might trim fields, apply defaults, remove private values, attach permissions, normalize roles, and shape data for an API request. The name sounds like a helper. The function behaves like a policy boundary.
The naming problem is not the word itself. The name is a symptom. The deeper issue is that the function does not have one clear responsibility, so no honest name fits.
This happens often in JavaScript because the language makes it easy to move quickly without forcing strong boundaries. Objects can grow loosely. Payloads can be shaped anywhere. Helper functions can accept anything. A generic name lets the code keep moving before the design is fully understood.
The failure mode appears later during change. A developer needs to adjust one part of the behavior, maybe only the API payload shape. They open prepareUser and discover that changing it might affect display formatting, permissions, caching, and analytics. The function name gave them no warning.
Good naming does not fix mixed responsibility.
It exposes it.
When no clear name exists, that is often the design telling you something. The code may need splitting. The responsibility may need to move. The function may be pretending that several different operations are one operation.
A bad name is not always the problem.
Sometimes it is the first honest signal that the problem is deeper.
Generic Data Names Make Wrong Assumptions Look Safe
JavaScript codebases often suffer from names like data, item, result, payload, response, and value.
These names are not always wrong. In tiny scopes, they can be perfectly fine. A two-line map callback does not need a dramatic name. A generic utility may genuinely work with any value.
The problem starts when generic names survive beyond generic code.
A variable called data inside an API boundary may represent a user profile, a billing record, a permissions object, a cached search result, or a partially transformed response. If the name does not tell the reader which shape they are holding, the reader starts guessing.
And guessing is where bugs begin.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A developer sees response.data and assumes it is the raw backend response. Another developer assumes it is already normalized. A third assumes it is safe to render directly. Someone adds a field based on one screen’s behavior. Another screen breaks because it depended on a slightly different shape.
The code did not fail because JavaScript allowed data.
It failed because the name erased the transformation history.
In many frontend systems, the same concept moves through several shapes. There may be a raw API response, a normalized client model, a form model, a display model, and an update payload. If all of them are called data, the codebase loses one of its most important safety signals.
The name should tell the reader where the value belongs in the flow.
There is a big difference between userResponse, userModel, userFormValues, userUpdatePayload, and visibleUserRows. Those names are longer, but they reduce guessing. They tell the next developer whether the value came from the server, belongs to the UI, is safe to mutate, or is ready to send back.
The hidden cost of generic data names is not that they make code less pretty.
They make wrong assumptions feel reasonable.
When developers cannot tell whether a value is raw, transformed, partial, cached, or user-edited, they write defensive code everywhere. They add optional chaining without understanding why the field may be missing. They duplicate mapping logic. They patch one screen while another keeps the old assumption.
A measured system would show this as extra debugging time, review comments, and repeated field-shape bugs.
Most teams never measure it.
They just call the codebase confusing.
Boolean Names Can Hide Product Policy
Boolean names look simple because they return true or false.
That simplicity can be misleading.
A boolean like isActive, isValid, isAllowed, hasAccess, or canEdit often sounds clear until the product grows. Then the name starts hiding policy decisions that are not binary in the way the code suggests.
What does isActive mean?
The user is not deleted? The subscription is paid? The email is verified? The workspace is not suspended? The account is inside a trial window? The user can log in? The user can appear in reports?
Each of those meanings might be reasonable in a different part of the system. If the codebase uses isActive for all of them, developers will eventually apply the wrong meaning in the wrong place.
This is not a naming rule problem. It is a domain language problem.
A boolean name becomes dangerous when it sounds like a complete truth but only represents one local interpretation. A function called canEditProject sounds like it owns the editing policy. If it only checks whether the current user is the owner, the name is too confident. It may ignore archived projects, locked workspaces, billing state, admin overrides, or field-level permissions.
The code may pass tests because the tests match the narrow implementation.
The name still misleads the system.
A better name sometimes needs to be more specific, even if it feels less elegant. isProjectOwner is narrower than canEditProject. hasPaidWorkspaceAccess is more honest than isActive. canEditProjectSettings is clearer than canEdit if the permission only applies to one action.
Specific names reduce accidental authority.
They tell developers what the code actually knows and what it does not know.
The failure mode is serious because boolean names often protect important behavior. Permissions, validation, account status, feature visibility, and workflow state are not places where guessing is cheap. If the name hides the real policy, the next developer may reuse it because it sounds right.
That is how one unclear boolean becomes a security bug, a broken UI state, or a customer support issue.
A boolean should not only answer true or false.
It should make the question clear.
Bad Function Names Make Side Effects Disappear
One of the worst naming problems in JavaScript is a function that sounds like a read but behaves like a write.
A function named getUserSession should not quietly refresh tokens, update local storage, mutate global state, and redirect the browser. A function named formatUser should not remove fields, apply permissions, and track analytics. A function named validateForm should not silently modify form values while checking them.
This is not about purity as a religious rule.
It is about trust.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When names hide side effects, developers stop being able to reason locally. They call one function and something changes somewhere else. A component rerenders. A cache value disappears. A cookie is rewritten. A request fires twice. A redirect happens during what looked like a harmless read.
The code becomes harder to debug because the suspicious behavior is not visible at the call site.
A small example shows the difference:
function getSession() {
const session = JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem("session"));
if (session?.expiresAt < Date.now()) {
localStorage.removeItem("session");
window.location.href = "/login";
}
return session;
}The name says getSession.
The function also clears storage and redirects the user.
That mismatch matters. A developer may call this inside a component render path, inside a data loader, inside a helper, or inside a test setup because the name sounds safe. The bug later appears as a random logout or unexpected redirect, and nobody suspects a getter.
A more honest design may separate reading from session enforcement. The exact structure depends on the app, but the name should prepare the reader for the behavior. readStoredSession and enforceValidSession communicate very different risks.
Names are part of the contract between the code and the reader.
If a function mutates, stores, clears, syncs, tracks, redirects, fetches, or retries, the name should not hide that cost. JavaScript applications already have enough implicit movement through closures, effects, caches, stores, browser APIs, and async flows. Naming should reduce surprise, not add more.
A bad function name does not only make code harder to read.
It makes dangerous behavior look harmless.
Fake Abstractions Usually Have Fake Names
Poor naming often appears when an abstraction is pretending to be more general than it really is.
You see a function called useTable, useForm, buildQuery, normalizeData, createConfig, or renderField. The name sounds reusable. It suggests a stable concept. It makes the code look mature.
Then you open it.
The abstraction knows about one product screen. It has conditions for one API. It handles a specific permission rule. It contains feature-specific labels. It has flags for special cases. It supports three options that only exist because different callers disagree.
The name says generic.
The implementation says local.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This mismatch is expensive because fake abstractions spread faster than obviously local code. Developers import them because the name invites reuse. A generic name lowers suspicion. Nobody feels guilty using useTable for another table. Nobody questions normalizeData if they need to normalize something. Nobody sees buildQuery and assumes it only fits one report page.
Then the abstraction starts collecting exceptions.
One table needs server pagination. Another needs client sorting. Another has permission-hidden columns. Another exports all rows, not v
